1	# Second Brain Wiki Configuration
2	
3	This file configures the skill for Evan's environment. Edit paths and
4	projects as your work evolves.
5	
6	---
7	
8	## Paths
9	
10	| Path | Value | Description |
11	|------|-------|-------------|
12	| Wiki directory | `~/second-brain/wiki/` | Where wiki pages live |
13	| Sources directory | `~/second-brain/sources/` | Raw source files stored here |
14	| Inbox directory | `~/Desktop/Second Brain Inbox/` | Drop files here for processing |
15	
16	---
17	
18	## Active Projects
19	
20	These are your current projects and areas of focus. The skill uses these
21	to find connections when ingesting new content.
22	
23	- **evaluator-super-agent** — automated prototype quality assessment using persona-driven walkthroughs, 7-dimension rubric scoring, actor/evaluator separation
24	- **prototype-creator** — generates clickable prototypes from RFEs, tiered AC classification, Playwright automation
25	- **agent-eval-harness** — framework for evaluating AI skill quality, eval.yaml configs, judges, scoring pipelines
26	- **ai-sdlc-pipeline** — three-phase pipeline: RFE Creator (WHAT) → Prototype Creator (SHOW) → Strat Creator (HOW)
27	- **my-cursor-claw** — personal workspace with 20+ skills, MCP connections, notetaking, automations
28	- **decision-kit** — Decision Driven Development toolkit, /strategize, /decide, /journal
29	- **automated-usability-testing** — simulated usability testing tool, persona YAML files, evaluate-flow protocol
30	- **archie** — UX research agent for the RHOAIUX team
31	- **rhoai-prototypes** — RHOAI prototype collection for evaluation
32	
33	---
34	
35	## Calendar Integration
36	
37	| Setting | Value |
38	|---------|-------|
39	| Calendar name | `primary` |
40	| Auto-ingest meetings | `true` |
41	| Minimum meeting duration | `15 min` |
42	| Require notes/transcript | `true` |
43	| Skip recurring without notes | `true` |
44	
45	Events that qualify for auto-ingestion:
46	- Have attached meeting notes or a description with substance (not just "Weekly sync")
47	- Have a linked Google Doc (agenda, transcript, action items)
48	- Duration is 15+ minutes
49	- Are not recurring meetings that had no notes added
50	
51	---
52	
53	## MCP Integrations
54	
55	| Integration | MCP Server | Used for |
56	|-------------|-----------|----------|
57	| Google Docs | `user-google-workspace` | Extracting doc content |
58	| Google Calendar | `user-google-workspace` | Meeting ingestion |
59	| Slack | `project-0-my-cursor-claw-slack` | Channel message ingestion |
60	
61	---
62	
63	## Cross-Link Priority
64	
65	When multiple connections exist, prioritize these categories (from the
66	wiki index):
67	
68	1. **Red Hat AI-SDLC** — pipeline components, RFE/prototype/strat flow
69	2. **Evaluation & Quality** — rubrics, heuristics, eval harness
70	3. **Tools & Skills** — decision kit, cursor claw, skill overlays
71	4. **Meetings** — meeting notes with action items and decisions
72	5. **Standups** — sprint progress and blockers
73	6. **Session Logs** — what was built and shipped
74

content:
1	# UX Quality Rubric
2	
3	The scoring system used by [Prototype Creator](prototype-creator.md) to evaluate generated prototypes. Scored by a restricted `prototype-scorer` agent (no shell, no network, no MCP).
4	
5	## Dimensions (0–2 each, 8 total)
6	
7	| Dimension | What It Measures |
8	|---|---|
9	| **Completeness** | Does the prototype cover the RFE's user stories and acceptance criteria? |
10	| **Usability** | Is the interaction pattern clear and free of obvious friction? (Nielsen's 10 heuristics) |
11	| **Feasibility** | Can this be built with the target design system (PatternFly 6)? |
12	| **Fidelity Match** | Does the prototype match the requested fidelity level — not over or under-engineered? |
13	
14	## Pass Threshold
15	
16	6+ total with no zeros. Prototypes scoring above get `prototype-creator-rubric-pass`. Below get `prototype-creator-needs-attention`.
17	
18	## Scoring Agent
19	
20	One invocation per dimension ensures scores are deterministic and auditable. The scorer agent is restricted — no shell, no network, no MCP — to prevent contamination.
21	
22	## Connection to Evaluator Super Agent
23	
24	The [Evaluator Super Agent](evaluator-super-agent.md) will extend this rubric with additional dimensions from simulated usability and desirability testing. See also [Nielsen's Heuristics](nielsens-heuristics-jagged-edge.md) for the research that informs the usability dimension.
25	
26	## Related
27	
28	- [Prototype Creator](prototype-creator.md) — the pipeline that uses this rubric
29	- [Evaluator Super Agent](evaluator-super-agent.md) — extends this scoring
30	- [Nielsen's Heuristics & The Jagged Edge](nielsens-heuristics-jagged-edge.md) — informs the usability dimension
31	- [Agent Eval Harness](agent-eval-harness.md) — similar pattern of rubric-based automated scoring
32	- [Weekly Design Sync — July 1](weekly-design-sync-2026-07-01.md) — scoring dimensions flagged for revisit at next meeting
33	
34	## Sources
35	
36	- sources/2026-06-09-prototype-creator-overview.html
37	- andybraren/prototype-creator config/ux-rubric.yaml
38

1	# Evaluator Super Agent
2	
3	Evan's primary assignment: build an evaluation agent that automates prototype quality assessment within the [Prototype Creator](prototype-creator.md) pipeline.
4	
5	**Status: Shipped.** The evaluator is a 3-phase skill pipeline on branch `evan/prototype-evaluate` at [andybraren/prototype-creator](https://github.com/andybraren/prototype-creator). It has evaluated 18+ STRATs against Jira acceptance criteria with Playwright persona walkthroughs.
6	
7	## What It Does (current)
8	
9	| Capability | Source | Status |
10	|---|---|---|
11	| AC evaluation | Custom Playwright persona journeys against Jira criteria | Shipped |
12	| Usability scoring | 7-dimension rubric with persona overlays (Zack Bodnar's framework) | Shipped |
13	| Consistency checking | Beau Morley's consistency checker (source + visual mode) | Shipped |
14	| Navigation reachability | Playwright verifies pages are clickable from sidebar | Shipped |
15	| Human review | Review tab in HTML report with override buttons + CSV writeback | Shipped |
16	| Report hosting | GitLab Pages (eval-reports-7ebc0e.pages.redhat.com) | Shipped |
17	| Google Sheet sync | Automated pass/fail + usability metrics to tracking sheet | Shipped |
18	| Feedback loop | Eval results → prototype-refine → re-eval (max 3 iterations) | In progress |
19	
20	## Architecture (confirmed June 16 with Andy)
21	
22	Evolved from "single monolithic super agent" to a **phased pipeline with overlays**:
23	
24	```
25	Phase 1: prototype-evaluate (extract ACs, RFE, Outcome, personas, journeys)
26	    ↓ writes extract-state.json
27	Phase 2: evaluate-journey (Playwright walkthroughs, consistency check)
28	    ↓ writes journey-log.json, screenshots, CSV
29	Phase 3: evaluate-usability (7-dimension persona scoring)
30	    ↓ updates journey-log.json
31	Phase 4: render-report.js + sync-sheet.js (HTML, CSV, Google Sheet)
32	```
33	
34	**Key design decisions:**
35	1. **Outcomes over Strategic Goals** as grounding criteria (Outcomes are consistently written)
36	2. **One Playwright pass** — shared screenshots across usability, consistency, and AC checks
37	3. **98% generic + 2% product overlay** (`config/product-overlay.yaml`) — portable to other products
38	4. **Hint mechanism** for dead-end navigation (failure logged but journey continues)
39	5. **Human-in-the-loop** — Review tab for designer input before feedback loop acts
40	
41	## Research References
42	
43	- **Avenir-UX** — autonomous UX evaluation agent using SEQ/SUS + persona framework
44	- **UXCascade** — multi-agent persona simulation with severity-rated issues (S1–S4)
45	- GitLab Notes REST API for MR comment posting
46	- Danger bot framework for CI-integrated review automation
47	
48	## How It Connects
49	
50	```
51	Jira STRAT (Outcome → RFE → STRAT)
52	    → prototype-create (generates code in workspace)
53	    → prototype-evaluate (this agent — 3 phases)
54	        ├── Phase 1: Extract ACs, personas, journeys from Jira
55	        ├── Phase 2: Playwright journeys + consistency check
56	        ├── Phase 3: Usability dimension scoring
57	        └── Phase 4: Report + Sheet sync
58	    → Human reviews report (override verdicts, add notes)
59	    → prototype-refine (acts on suggestions + human annotations)
60	    → Re-evaluate (loop until PASS or max iterations)
61	    → prototype-submit (MR to GitLab)
62	```
63	
64	## Key Design Inputs from Demo (May 14)
65	
66	From the [Prototype Creator Demo](prototype-creator-demo-may14.md):
67	- Consistency checkers should only evaluate what's NEW (CSS class="new" tagging)
68	- Even auto-mode should produce decision-kit pages for post-review
69	- Evaluator needs outcome-level awareness, not just feature-level
70	- Preview URL is the evaluation entry point (load the MR URL, assess in browser context)
71	- Token efficiency matters — scope evaluation to changed areas only
72	
73	## Related
74	
75	- [Outcome Tiger Team](outcome-tiger-team.md) — proposes outcome-level awareness for evaluation, directly extending this agent's scope
76	- [Prototype Creator Demo — May 14](prototype-creator-demo-may14.md) — walkthrough with design insights for this agent
77	- [Prototype Creator](prototype-creator.md) — the pipeline this plugs into
78	- [UX Quality Rubric](ux-quality-rubric.md) — existing scoring to extend
79	- [Agent Eval Harness](agent-eval-harness.md) — pattern for automated eval loops
80	- [Decision Kit](decision-kit.md) — using /strategize to plan this agent
81	- [Nielsen's Heuristics & The Jagged Edge](nielsens-heuristics-jagged-edge.md) — informs methodology choice
82	- [My Cursor Claw](my-cursor-claw.md) — the workspace where this will be built
83	- [Ansible AI Helpers Strategy — 2026-06-17](ansible-ai-helpers-strategy-2026-06-17.md) — heuristic eval pilot connects to automated evaluation vision
84	
85	## Updates from June 16, 2026 Review
86	
87	Decisions made with Andy Braren (see [Andy/Evan Eval Review — June 16](andy-evan-eval-review-2026-06-16.md)):
88	
89	- **Architecture confirmed:** Centralized super evaluator, not fragmented skills. One Playwright pass, shared screenshots.
90	- **Grounding confirmed:** Outcomes as primary criteria, Strategic Goals excluded for now.
91	- **Overlay approach adopted:** 98% generic + 2% product-specific layer. See [GitLab skill-overlays best practice](https://gitlab.cee.redhat.com/global-engineering/wg-agentic-sdlc/-/blob/main/best-practices/skill-overlays.md).
92	- **Hint mechanism shipped:** Simulated UX researcher provides next URL on failure, allowing evaluation to continue while logging failure impact.
93	- **Screenshot consolidation:** Sub-agents share one set of Playwright screenshots instead of each capturing independently.
94	
95	- [RH AI-First UX — 2026-06-18](rh-ai-first-ux-2026-06-18.md) — Evan demoed automated usability testing + PatternFly consistency checker to full team
96	- [Andy/Evan Meeting — June 22](andy-evan-2026-06-22.md) — eval iteration history, flagging system, Prototype Creator integration strategy
97	- [AI-First Sync — June 25](ai-first-sync-2026-06-25.md) — Evan's full evaluator presentation to the team, readiness check discussion
98	- [RH AI-First UX — June 25](rh-ai-first-ux-2026-06-25.md) — testing fragmentation concerns, unified testing proposal
99	- [Standup — June 29](standup-2026-06-29.md) — 2 automated usability testing agents in development
100	- [Skill Overlays](skill-overlays.md) — context engineering technique relevant to team-specific overlays
101	- [Prompt Engineering Best Practices](prompt-engineering-best-practices.md) — sequential prompting and format-controlled output underpin the 3-phase evaluation pipeline
102	- [Andy/Evan Meeting — June 29](andy-evan-2026-06-29.md) — findings tab, auto-fix vs report-only modes, X-ray vs blind evaluation, centralized dashboard
103	- [Midpoint Check-in — June 30](midpoint-checkin-2026-06-30.md) — Zack: "pioneering work" combining research + AI + SDLC; report must be immediately actionable for designers
104	- [Weekly Design Sync — July 1](weekly-design-sync-2026-07-01.md) — Evan to demo evaluator next week; Nielsen's heuristics confirmed as base rubric
105	
106	## Sources
107	
108	- [Andy/Evan Eval Review — June 16](andy-evan-eval-review-2026-06-16.md)
109	- Andy/Evan meeting notes — 2026-06-08
110	- sources/2026-06-09-prototype-creator-overview.html
111	- /strategize session — 2026-06-09 (in progress)
112

1	# Nielsen's Heuristics & The Jagged Edge
2	
3	First hands-on internship assignment: apply Nielsen Norman's 10 Usability Heuristics to Kubernetes-style interfaces across multiple AI tools and compare their analysis quality.
4	
5	## The Jagged Edge
6	
7	Research from Harvard Business School shows AI capabilities aren't uniform — models are very good at some tasks and surprisingly bad at others. The boundary between "good" and "bad" isn't obvious. This is the "jagged technological frontier."
8	
9	## What Was Tested
10	
11	- Selected Kubernetes/OpenShift UI screens at different complexity levels
12	- Crafted evaluation prompts asking each tool to assess against all 10 heuristics with severity ratings (1–4)
13	- Ran the same prompt through Cursor, Claude Code, Gemini (Flash + Pro), and ChatGPT
14	
15	## Comparison Dimensions
16	
17	- Quality of analysis — did it correctly identify real usability issues?
18	- Depth — surface-level observations vs. genuine insight?
19	- Specificity — generic advice vs. grounded in the actual interface?
20	- Structure — how well-organized and actionable?
21	- Hallucinations — did it make things up?
22	- Unique insights — did any tool catch something others missed?
23	
24	## Deliverables
25	
26	- [Jaquez - Task 1 - Nielsen & Agents](https://docs.google.com/document/d/11o9Px8wddeal3Ap7vBVtyDSw8FWaMGsuTYQwyc0hSAQ) (Google Doc)
27	- [Update — Evan Jaquez](https://docs.google.com/presentation/d/1AoUZtCCJf4btr68jOY3_xKbJU2wyx6XTqjGn6IIRd3w) (Google Slides)
28	
29	## Relevance to Current Work
30	
31	The comparison methodology directly informs the [Evaluator Super Agent](evaluator-super-agent.md) — understanding which AI tools produce research-quality heuristic evaluations vs. shallow summaries is critical for choosing the right approach to automated prototype evaluation.
32	
33	## Related
34	
35	- [UX Quality Rubric](ux-quality-rubric.md) — rubric-based scoring approach
36	- [Evaluator Super Agent](evaluator-super-agent.md) — applies these learnings
37	- [Prototype Creator](prototype-creator.md) — where evaluations will be used
38	- [Ansible AI Helpers Strategy — 2026-06-17](ansible-ai-helpers-strategy-2026-06-17.md) — heuristic eval skill selected as Ansible pilot
39	- [Standup — June 29](standup-2026-06-29.md) — usability comparison approach being iterated on
40	- [RH AI-First UX — June 25](rh-ai-first-ux-2026-06-25.md) — unified testing strategy connects to heuristic methodology
41	- [Weekly Design Sync — July 1](weekly-design-sync-2026-07-01.md) — team agreed to adopt Nielsen's heuristics as base rubric for evaluations
42	
43	## Sources
44	
45	- sources/2026-06-03-nielsen-agents-task1.html
46

1	# Ansible AI Helpers Strategy — 2026-06-17
2	
3	Strategy session on integrating the UXD AI Helpers repo with Ansible's Carbonite harness. First major external consumer of the centralized skill distribution model.
4	
5	## Attendees
6	
7	Jeremy Perry, Mike Franke, Nicole Thoen, Jeff Puzzo, Cate Ferman, Leslie Hinson, Andy Braren, Melissa Grimes (invited: Taufique Rahman)
8	
9	## Context
10	
11	- Ansible uses **Carbonite** as their AI harness, managed by the **ATT team** (thinly veiled Star Wars reference)
12	- Jeff Puzzo and Nicole Thoen have been working with the Carbonite team on PatternFly integration — PatternFly is already in Carbonite's internal AI marketplace
13	- A readout with Ansible leadership the previous week surfaced interest in a **heuristic evaluation skill** as a concrete example of UX expertise
14	- Tracy (UXD leadership) advised the team to bring an opinionated point of view — not just "here are skills" but "here's how UXD recommends using them"
15	
16	## Key Decisions
17	
18	### 1. Heuristic Eval Selected as Pilot
19	The [heuristic evaluation skill](nielsens-heuristics-jagged-edge.md) was selected as the initial pilot to demonstrate AI Helpers integration with Carbonite. Cate Ferman will coordinate a 2-3 session pilot with Ansible design and research teams to validate recommendations before broader rollout. Questions about guardrails, cadence, and usage guidelines are deferred until after pilot validation.
20	
21	### 2. Centralized Repo Integration via MRs
22	Nicole Thoen was firm: skills must flow through the AI Helpers repo via merge requests, not ad-hoc downloads. This preserves UXD ownership and distribution control. If teams pull skills locally and fork them, "they're not ours anymore." Jira tickets will track integration progress and give external teams visibility into timelines.
23	
24	### 3. June 30 Presentation to Ansible Leadership
25	Jeremy Perry secured a 30-minute slot with Kevin (Ansible technical leadership) and his staff. The pitch: UXD's opinionated design pipeline ("the purple box") — not just access to skills, but a recommended way of working. Key contacts on the Ansible side: Tim Puyer and Nathan Weatherly (global engineering working group).
26	
27	## The "Purple Box" Discussion
28	
29	Tracy raised an important framing question: teams shouldn't just get access to skills — they need UXD's point of view on how to use them effectively within their workflows. This is the [AI-SDLC Pipeline](ai-sdlc-pipeline.md) in practice. The challenge: each product team's orchestrator is different (Carbonite for Ansible, something else for OCP), so the mechanism for consuming skills varies even if the skills themselves are maintained centrally.
30	
31	## Scaling Concerns
32	
33	Nicole and Mike acknowledged the maintenance burden: per-orchestrator integration means each new product team is "square one" for wiring. OCP's Matt Carlton already reached out about getting skills into their AISDLC. Possible model: UXD people embedded in each product space own the orchestrator-specific integration while central team maintains the skills.
34	
35	Jeff proposed filling workflow gaps proactively — identify what Ansible's harness is missing, show up with a working solution, and create "I want that" moments rather than asking teams what they want.
36	
37	## Next Steps
38	
39	| Owner | Action |
40	|---|---|
41	| Melissa Grimes | Submit MR to add heuristic eval skill into AI Helpers repo |
42	| Jeff Puzzo | Configure technical connection between Carbonite and AI Helpers repo |
43	| Nicole Thoen | Create Jira tickets for AI Helpers integration (visibility for Ansible team) |
44	| Jeremy Perry + Mike Franke | Coordinate follow-up meeting next week |
45	| Cate Ferman + Nick Lemon | Prepare pilot plan presentation for Ansible team |
46	
47	## Internship Connection
48	
49	This meeting directly demonstrates the UXD skill distribution model that the [AI-SDLC Pipeline](ai-sdlc-pipeline.md) describes. The heuristic eval pilot connects to the same [Nielsen's Heuristics](nielsens-heuristics-jagged-edge.md) methodology from Evan's first internship task. The [Agent Eval Harness](agent-eval-harness.md) patterns (eval.yaml, judges, automated scoring) will likely inform how pilot results are measured. The [Prototype Creator](prototype-creator.md) pipeline is a natural next candidate for Ansible integration after the heuristic eval pilot proves the distribution model.
50	
51	## Related
52	
53	- [AI-SDLC Pipeline](ai-sdlc-pipeline.md) — the "purple box" opinionated pipeline being pitched to Ansible
54	- [Nielsen's Heuristics & The Jagged Edge](nielsens-heuristics-jagged-edge.md) — the heuristic eval skill selected as pilot
55	- [Agent Eval Harness](agent-eval-harness.md) — evaluation patterns relevant to pilot measurement
56	- [Prototype Creator](prototype-creator.md) — next candidate for Ansible integration
57	- [Evaluator Super Agent](evaluator-super-agent.md) — automated evaluation connects to the heuristic eval pilot
58	- [My Cursor Claw](my-cursor-claw.md) — the AI helpers repo and skills being distributed
59	- [WG: UXD Efficiency — June 29](wg-uxd-efficiency-2026-06-29.md) — continuation of Ansible integration work, AI helpers as primary vetted skills source
60	
61	## Sources
62	
63	- ~/Desktop/Notes Inbox/gemini-2026-06-17-sharing-ai-helpers-strategy-with-ansible---20260617-1329-edt---notes-by-gemini.txt
64

1	# RH AI-First UX -- June 25, 2026
2	
3	Weekly AI-first UX team meeting covering Outcome Creator, testing strategy, and infrastructure alignment.
4	
5	## Key Discussion Points
6	
7	- **Outcome Creator JTBD integration:** Yahav tested Jobs to be Done data in Outcome Creator. Successfully linked specific jobs to outcomes. Bug: skill writes artifact files to project root instead of workspace.
8	- **Personnel update:** Beau on medical leave for several months. Team adjusting Kiwi-related work.
9	- **Quality Engineering transition:** QE team transitioning under Ron's management. Yahav got handover doc with context on UXD-within-QE initiative. Kamesh facilitating intro.
10	- **Agentic CI and skill orchestration:** Need for a higher-level orchestrator that determines which skills are executed within the RHAI pipeline. Zack shared "Agentic CI" docs (library for packaging skills into agentic packages).
11	- **Infrastructure standardization:** Lack of clarity in mapping between different infrastructure concepts (blue box, implementation zone). UIE aims to standardize.
12	- **Models of service outcomes:** Andy used Outcome Creator for models of service/inference outcomes. Planning to present at PM-wide meeting next Wednesday. PMs appreciated JTBD data integration.
13	- **Testing fragmentation:** Zack flagged proliferation of varied prototype/usability testing tools across the org (Yonyi's team, prototype evaluator, heuristic analysis skills). Team needs to establish canonical version or unified standard.
14	- **Unified testing proposal:** Yahav assigned to draft formal proposal for review next week defining dimensions and requirements for standard usability testing approach.
15	- **Artifact storage pattern:** Team agreed to establish a common design pattern for storing project artifacts consistently across all skills.
16	
17	## Decisions
18	
19	- Common artifact storage pattern to be established across skills
20	- Unified usability testing strategy proposal to be drafted
21	- Journeys and milestones reside within outcome level in Jira architecture
22	
23	## Related
24	
25	- [Outcome Tiger Team](outcome-tiger-team.md) -- outcome creator progress and JTBD integration
26	- [Evaluator Super Agent](evaluator-super-agent.md) -- part of the testing fragmentation discussion
27	- [AI-SDLC Pipeline](ai-sdlc-pipeline.md) -- agentic CI and skill orchestration
28	- [Prototype Creator](prototype-creator.md) -- artifact storage pattern
29	- [Nielsen's Heuristics & The Jagged Edge](nielsens-heuristics-jagged-edge.md) -- unified testing strategy context
30	
31	## Sources
32	
33	- ~/Desktop/Notes Inbox/gemini-2026-06-25-rh-ai-first-ux---20260625-1600-edt---notes-by-gemini.txt
34

text:
Here's what your wiki knows about usability testing:

---

## Direct matches

**[Nielsen's Heuristics & The Jagged Edge](nielsens-heuristics-jagged-edge.md)**
Your first internship task — applied Nielsen Norman's 10 Usability Heuristics to Kubernetes/OpenShift UI screens and compared how well Cursor, Claude Code, Gemini, and ChatGPT perform heuristic evaluation. Key finding: AI tools have a "jagged edge" — strong at some usability tasks, surprisingly weak at others. Deliverables are in a Google Doc and Slides deck. This research directly informs your evaluator methodology.

**[UX Quality Rubric](ux-quality-rubric.md)**
The 8-dimension scoring system (0–2 each) used to evaluate prototypes. The **Usability** dimension is explicitly grounded in Nielsen's 10 heuristics. Pass threshold is 6+ with no zeros. Evaluation is agent-restricted to prevent contamination.

**[Evaluator Super Agent](evaluator-super-agent.md)**
Your primary shipped project. Three-phase pipeline for automated prototype usability assessment:
- Phase 1: Extract ACs, personas, journeys from Jira
- Phase 2: Playwright walkthroughs + consistency check
- Phase 3: 7-dimension usability scoring using Zack Bodnar's persona overlay framework
- Phase 4: HTML report + Google Sheet sync

Key design choices: Outcomes (not Strategic Goals) as grounding criteria, one shared Playwright pass, 98% generic + 2% product-specific overlay, hint mechanism for dead-end navigation, human review tab with override buttons.

Cate Ferman is coordinating 2-3 sessions with Ansible design and research teams to validate it before broader rollout.

**Upcoming demo** ([Weekly Design Sync — July 1](weekly-design-sync-2026-07-01.md)): You're scheduled to demo the Evaluator Super Agent the week of July 7. The team agreed Nielsen's 10 heuristics is the canonical base rubric. Scoring dimensions are flagged for revisit at the next meeting.

**Research references in the evaluator**: Avenir-UX (autonomous eval with SEQ/SUS) and UXCascade (multi-agent persona simulation, S1–S4 severity ratings) informed the architecture.
